title: A Gauge-Covariant Theoretical Framework for Non-Abelian Holonomy Estimation and Feed-Forward Correction in Time-Bin Photonic Qudits
abstract: We develop a theoretical and computational framework for estimating and correcting non-Abelian geometric distortions in time-bin photonic qudit processing when the relevant encoded object is a transported logical subspace rather than a collection of independent rays. In such settings, for example under mode mixing, multiplexed routing, or effective degeneracies, the geometric contribution is naturally matrix-valued and is described by a Wilczek-Zee holonomy on a rank-$m$ sub-bundle of the ambient Hilbert space. The framework generalises prior Abelian time-bin Pancharatnam-Berry feed-forward calibration, in which geometric distortions are represented by bin-resolved scalar phases, to the non-Abelian, matrix-valued case. We construct a gauge-covariant discrete estimator from overlap matrices between successive subspace frames: the polar factor of each overlap gives a unitary backward frame comparator, and the adjoint comparators compose to approximate the forward path-ordered exponential of the Wilczek-Zee connection. We prove gauge covariance under frame changes, polar optimality of the local comparator, consistency under partition refinement, and perturbative stability under well-conditioned overlap errors. We then formulate left- and right-acting feed-forward correction rules for removing the estimated holonomy from an effective logical operation. The work does not assume a device-specific transfer matrix, loss model, detector model, or experimental calibration pipeline; numerical studies use synthetic non-Abelian transport models to validate covariance, convergence, conditioning dependence, and correction fidelity.
summary: The paper develops a new way to calibrate quantum gates that suffer from non-Abelian geometric errors, specifically for photonic qudits. It replaces simple phase subtraction with a matrix-based method that is robust even when the choice of basis changes. This allows for more accurate error correction in complex, high-dimensional quantum computing architectures.
main_problem: Estimating and correcting non-Abelian geometric distortions (Wilczek-Zee holonomies) in quantum systems where the encoded object is a transported logical subspace rather than independent rays.
main_result: A gauge-covariant theoretical framework for reconstructing non-Abelian holonomy using polar decomposition of overlap matrices, providing robust left- and right-acting feed-forward correction rules.
method: A discrete estimator constructed from the polar decomposition of overlap matrices between successive subspace frames, validated through numerical studies using synthetic non-Abelian transport models.
model_or_system: Time-bin photonic qudits where a rank-m logical subspace is subject to transport along a path in parameter space, potentially involving mode mixing or effective degeneracies.
key_observables: Matrix-valued holonomies, spectrum of the holonomy, Wilson-loop traces, and the minimum singular value of overlap matrices as a conditioning diagnostic.
important_parameters: Rank m of the subspace, control parameters lambda, and the conditioning parameter mu_min (minimum singular value).
main_assumptions: The reconstruction assumes well-conditioned overlap matrices (singular values bounded away from zero) and a smooth (C2) frame path; it does not assume a specific device-specific transfer matrix or loss model.
figures_summary: Figure 1 outlines the reconstruction algorithm; Figure 2 shows convergence of Frobenius error with refinement; Figure 3/6 includes a diagnostic for projector-step refinement.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the failure of Abelian calibration in subspace architectures, proposes a matrix-valued gauge-covariant alternative, provides mathematical proofs for optimality and stability, and validates the framework via numerical simulations of synthetic transport models.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for quantum optics and photonic QI as it provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for calibrating high-dimensional, non-Abelian geometric gates in complex photonic circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: Aggregate convergence validation. The plot shows Frobenius errors for the non- Abelian connection benchmark, frame-overlap pipeline, holonomy estimation for correction, and left/right feed-forward correction.
Figure 3: Aggregate unitarity validation. The reconstructed holonomies and corrected gates remain unitary to numerical precision across the tested refinements.
Figure 4: Feed-forward correction infidelity 1 −F. Plotting infidelity rather than fidelity resolves convergence near unit fidelity.

Figure 6: Projector-step refinement diagnos- tic for the frame-overlap pipeline. The maxi- mum adjacent-projector distance decreases un- der mesh refinement, consistent with the re- quirement that nearby overlap matrices remain well-conditioned.
Figure 5: Scaled-noise slopes across controlled conditioning levels. The slopes cluster near one, consistent with approximately linear de- pendence on η/µmin in the perturbative regime.
